---
title: "Slide Style Preview"
subtitle: "Three blue-green directions for Applied Math Lab"
format:
  revealjs:
    css:
      - amlab-style-previews.css
---

## What I Tested

::: {.grid-3}
::: {.card}
### Quarto patterns
Gradient section slides, multi-column layouts, and code line highlighting from the Quarto RevealJS docs.
:::

::: {.card}
### RevealJS patterns
Large fit-text headlines, stacked fragments, and visual panels that reveal ideas in stages.
:::

::: {.card}
### Course constraint
An academic tone with the same blue-green family as the site, but with more variation in slide rhythm.
:::
:::

## How to Read the Preview

::: {.grid-3}
::: {.card}
### Option A
Bright, calm, and lecture-first. Best when mathematics and diagrams need to stay readable.
:::

::: {.card}
### Option B
More editorial and energetic. Best when you want stronger section transitions and more visual momentum.
:::

::: {.card}
### Option C
Cleaner and more technical. Best when code, derivations, and worked calculations dominate the session.
:::
:::

# Option A: Coastal Lecture {background-gradient="linear-gradient(135deg, #0d3556 0%, #12706d 100%)" .section-break footer=false}

## Hero + Structure {.option-tide}

<div class="preview-kicker">Option A · soft gradient · theory-first</div>

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
<div class="tide-hero">
<div class="hero-band">A light, open layout for derivations, conceptual figures, and guided interpretation.</div>
<h2 class="r-fit-text tide-headline">From equations to patterns</h2>
<p>The slide breathes. Important formulas stay central, supporting commentary stays compact, and the visual hierarchy remains stable across the deck.</p>
</div>
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
<div class="stat-column">
  <div class="stat-chip"><span class="stat-num">1</span><span>main idea per slide</span></div>
  <div class="stat-chip"><span class="stat-num">2</span><span>supporting panels max</span></div>
  <div class="stat-chip"><span class="stat-num">3</span><span>visual anchors to guide reading</span></div>
</div>
:::
::::

## Theory + Visual {.option-tide}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.formula-panel}
### Representative layout

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = d_u \Delta u + f(u,v), \qquad
\frac{\partial v}{\partial t} = d_v \Delta v + g(u,v)
$$

Keep the formula in a dedicated panel, then place the explanatory text beneath it or beside it.
:::
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    A[Homogeneous equilibrium] --> B[Jacobian]
    B --> C[Mode matrix A_n]
    C --> D[Growth rates sigma_n]
    D --> E[Predicted spatial pattern]
```

::: {.caption-panel}
The figure gets its own surface, so the slide still looks composed even when the mathematics is dense.
:::
:::
::::

## Quantitative Slide {.option-tide}

<div class="preview-kicker">Growth-rate visual without clutter</div>

<div class="mode-chart">
  <div class="mode-row"><span class="mode-label">n = 1</span><div class="bar-track"><div class="bar-fill" style="width: 18%"></div></div><span class="mode-value">-0.03</span></div>
  <div class="mode-row"><span class="mode-label">n = 3</span><div class="bar-track"><div class="bar-fill" style="width: 32%"></div></div><span class="mode-value">0.01</span></div>
  <div class="mode-row"><span class="mode-label">n = 5</span><div class="bar-track"><div class="bar-fill highlight" style="width: 74%"></div></div><span class="mode-value">0.021</span></div>
  <div class="mode-row"><span class="mode-label">n = 6</span><div class="bar-track"><div class="bar-fill" style="width: 69%"></div></div><span class="mode-value">0.020</span></div>
  <div class="mode-row"><span class="mode-label">n = 8</span><div class="bar-track"><div class="bar-fill" style="width: 20%"></div></div><span class="mode-value">-0.01</span></div>
</div>

::: {.caption-panel}
This direction is the safest choice if you want the whole deck family to feel polished, calm, and easy to read.
:::

# Option B: Current Atlas {background-gradient="linear-gradient(135deg, #0a2744 0%, #0f4f81 50%, #159a8c 100%)" .section-break footer=false}

## Asymmetric Narrative {.option-current}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="34%"}
<div class="side-rail">
  <div class="rail-label">Option B · editorial layout</div>
  <h3>Use a bold thesis zone</h3>
  <p>Each slide starts with a strong left rail, then the right side changes shape between evidence, figures, and takeaways.</p>
</div>
:::

::: {.column width="66%"}
<div class="dark-grid">
  <div class="dark-card">
    <h3>Slide rhythm</h3>
    <p>Alternate thesis, visual evidence, and conclusion instead of repeating the same card grid on every slide.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="dark-card">
    <h3>Section contrast</h3>
    <p>Use dark blue section dividers with teal accents so the deck feels more cinematic when moving across topics.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="dark-card wide">
    <h3>Best use</h3>
    <p>Networks, collective motion, Gray-Scott, and any session where animation, pattern change, or layered interpretation matters.</p>
  </div>
</div>
:::
::::

## Layered Visual Reveal {.option-current}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="56%"}
<div class="r-stack heatmap-stack">
  <div class="heatmap-box"></div>
  <div class="chip chip-a fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="0">Seed perturbation</div>
  <div class="chip chip-b fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="1">Boundary choice</div>
  <div class="chip chip-c fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="2">Dominant mode</div>
</div>
:::

::: {.column width="44%"}
<div class="dark-callout fragment" data-fragment-index="0">
  <h3>Stage 1</h3>
  <p>Show the field first.</p>
</div>
<div class="dark-callout fragment" data-fragment-index="1">
  <h3>Stage 2</h3>
  <p>Reveal the modeling choice that changes the spectrum.</p>
</div>
<div class="dark-callout fragment" data-fragment-index="2">
  <h3>Stage 3</h3>
  <p>End with the interpretation, not with the raw image alone.</p>
</div>
:::
::::

## Strong Closing Slide {.option-current}

<div class="current-banner">A deck like this feels more dynamic because the layout changes with the argument.</div>

::: {.grid-3}
::: {.tool-card .current-card}
### Stronger breaks
Section transitions feel intentional.
:::

::: {.tool-card .current-card}
### Better pacing
Fragments and layers help you speak through the logic.
:::

::: {.tool-card .current-card}
### Higher risk
It needs discipline, or it can become too heavy.
:::
:::

# Option C: Delta Notebook {background-gradient="linear-gradient(135deg, #123b60 0%, #0f6d73 100%)" .section-break footer=false}

## Clean Analytical Grid {.option-delta}

<div class="preview-kicker">Option C · notebook-journal hybrid · calculation-first</div>

<div class="delta-shell">
  <div class="delta-tag">Why it works</div>
  <table class="delta-table">
    <thead>
      <tr><th>Need</th><th>Visual answer</th></tr>
    </thead>
    <tbody>
      <tr><td>Dense derivation</td><td>Sharp white background with blue rule and clear spacing</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Algorithm explanation</td><td>Two-column code and interpretation layout</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Worked example</td><td>Small result panels that stay aligned</td></tr>
    </tbody>
  </table>
</div>

## Code + Interpretation {.option-delta}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="56%"}
```python {code-line-numbers="|1-2|4-5|7-8"}
lam = -(n * np.pi / L) ** 2
A_n = J + lam * D
sigma = np.linalg.eigvals(A_n)

if np.max(sigma.real) > 0:
    unstable.append((n, np.max(sigma.real)))

dominant = max(unstable, key=lambda item: item[1])
print(dominant)
```
:::

::: {.column width="44%"}
<div class="delta-shell compact">
  <div class="delta-tag">Interpretation</div>
  <ul>
    <li>The blue rule keeps the slide visually anchored.</li>
    <li>The code block is crisp rather than decorative.</li>
    <li>The right panel holds the verbal interpretation students need.</li>
  </ul>
</div>
<div class="result-chip">Dominant mode: n = 5</div>
:::
::::

## Decision Guide {.option-delta}

::: {.grid-3}
::: {.choice-card}
### Choose A
If the priority is clarity across all sessions, especially theory-heavy PDE and ODE lectures.
:::

::: {.choice-card}
### Choose B
If you want a more distinctive and energetic lecture experience with stronger visual pacing.
:::

::: {.choice-card}
### Choose C
If you want the cleanest environment for technical sessions with equations, code, and worked calculations.
:::
:::

::: {.caption-panel}
My recommendation is to choose one base direction for the whole course, then borrow isolated layout ideas from the others. Option A is the safest base. Option B is the strongest visual leap. Option C is the clearest technical format.
:::
